sourceMovie.title: Scream
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Scream (1996) as a crime theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The crime and horror blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: I Am Not a Serial Killer
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Scream clicked for you, I Am Not a Serial Killer (2016) is a strong next stop: both trade in crime and horror, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Scream clicked for you, Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark (2019) is a strong next stop: both trade in horror and mystery, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Scream?
faq.answer: Try I Am Not a Serial Killer, Scary Stories to Tell in the Dark, Fear Street: 1994—each shares DNA with Scream while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Scream?
faq.answer: Scream (1996) is best described as Crime, Horror, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Scream part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Scream Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
